risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We will randomly allocate practices to the intervention or usual care arm. We will pair practices by size ... and by whether they have implemented the DIAMOND ... program. Within each pair, we will randomize practices to either: 1. the use of Depression Medication Choice ... or 2. usual care. A study statistician will perform the randomization centrally after the practices have been enrolled" Comment: The study explicitly states that practices will be randomly allocated and randomized centrally by a statistician. Although the exact random number method is not named, the wording indicates a proper random allocation process rather than a predictable or non-random rule, which is sufficient for Low risk under this domain.

Quote: "A study statistician will perform the randomization centrally after the practices have been enrolled and ensure concealment of allocation." Comment: This explicitly states central randomization and that allocation concealment will be ensured, which indicates recruiters could not foresee upcoming assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Practices, clinicians and investigators will not be masked to allocation. ... The intervention will consist of the use of the Depression Medication Choice decision aid by patients and their primary care clinician during the clinical encounter." Comment: Participants and personnel were explicitly not masked, and the intervention required visible use of the decision aid during the clinical encounter. Outcomes include patient-reported decisional conflict, knowledge, satisfaction, preference, involvement, depression outcomes, and medication adherence, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation and the accompanying change in clinician behavior.

Quote: "Practices, clinicians and investigators will not be masked to allocation. However, patients will remain unaware of the study’s main hypotheses." and "We will collect patients’ data through: 1. self-reported questionnaires administered before and after the clinical encounter with their clinician, and at 3 and 6 months post-encounter" and "Patients will complete the decisional conflict scale (DCS) immediately after the clinical encounter" and "Patients will complete the nine items of the PHQ-9 ... as a measure of the severity of depression symptoms" Comment: The outcomes were assessed mainly by patient self-report, so the patient is effectively the assessor. Because patients were not reported to be blinded to allocation (only unaware of the study’s main hypotheses), knowledge of receiving the decision aid could influence subjective outcomes such as decisional conflict, satisfaction, knowledge, preference, and PHQ-9 symptom ratings. Although some outcomes were also objective (e.g., pharmacy records) and some were assessed using video-based independent observation (OPTION), the study reports a mix of subjective and objective outcomes; under the worst-outcome rule, the unblinded subjective patient-reported outcomes make this domain high risk.

Quote: "To prevent loss to follow-up and support the intention-to-treat principle, we will centrally follow patients, and ensure that patient surveys and pharmacy follow-ups are complete." Comment: This is a study protocol, so no actual attrition numbers are reported yet. However, the paper explicitly states procedures intended to prevent loss to follow-up and complete follow-up for the relevant patient outcomes, and it specifies repeated follow-up at 3 and 6 months with centralized tracking. There is no evidence in the text of substantial missing outcome data, differential attrition, or inappropriate handling of missingness; given the absence of reported losses and the stated follow-up procedures, the best judgement from this source is low risk.
